## The Basics

The United States Department of the Interior Bureau of Reclamation is the government agency most associated with the measurement of water in the open ditches that characterize older irrigation districts across the west. The [Water Measurement Manual (WMM)](https://www.usbr.gov/tsc/techreferences/mands/wmm/) (@WMM2001USBR) is commonly referenced is many discussions I have been part of. For now I want to just define the various forms of weirs and note which are common here in CO.

### Weir Types

Below are some of the standard weir types. Cipoletti and rectangular weirs are the most common in the laterals and private laterals in the COID system.

```{figure} ../Images/weirTypes.png
:label: Basic Weir Types
:alt: Basic Weir Types
:width: 600 px
:align: center

Basic Weir Types: [source: ](https://learn.hydrologystudio.com/studio-express/knowledge-base/weirs/) 
```
### Nomenclature

There is some general nomenclature for the various features of the water flow over the weir. Those general labels are shown below.

```{figure} ../Images/WeirLabels.png
:label: Weir Labels
:alt: Weir Labels
:width: 500 px
:align: center

Image from @Sinclair2021FlowSharpCrested
```
h or H is called the head and is measured to the calm water upstream of the weir (4h is recommended). The for assumption is that the flow velocity ($\bar{u}$) in the pool above the weir is negligable. It is generally assumed that $\bar{u} \approx 0$. For a flow velocity of 1 ft/s the correction to the head would be 0.19 in or 0.015 ft. P, the height of the weir crest above the channel bottom, is important because we are generally trying to keep h/P > 0.2 where the tables are most applicable. 

Many of the weirs I have seen in this area are Cipoletti Weirs that are either 1 or 2 feet wide at the crest. On the other hand as I explore more along the ditches I am finding more rectangular, uncontracted, weirs where the barrier is perpendicular and formed from 2x material. I suspect this doesn't quite qualify as a 'sharp' weir though perhaps it's close. Below is shown a gauge plate that one of the COID ditch riders was carrying that orginally came from the Bureau of Reclamation (perhaps).

### Flow Measurement (Ideal)

The flow in the ditch is generally determined by measuring h, the head, and looking up the flow in a table. This will be explored more in a subsequent page. The core difficulty is that the easy place to measure the head is directly above the crest, labelled Y in {fig}`Weir Labels`, which is **NOT** the same as h. Ideally a gauge stick is placed in the calm water before the crest of the weir and it is calibrated so that 0 is level with the crest. The reality is that Y is what is usually measured and is assumed to be an acceptable substitute for h. This is not completely nuts since putting a staff in the flow on the crest will tends to back the water up indicating a water level higher than Y. It's just not clear how close to h it actually is.

### Flow Measurement (Realistic)

More realistically we measure the head using a gauge staff which is a 'stick' that is placed on the crest of the weir and the height of the water backed up against the staff is taken to be the head h. Below is a gauge plate used by a COID ditch rider that they believe originally came from the Bureau of Reclamation (the folks responsible for @WMM2001USBR).

```{figure} ../Images/GaugePlate.jpeg
:label: USBR Gauge Plate from COID
:alt: USBR Gauge Plate from COID
:width: 200 px
:align: center

USBR Gauge Plate for reference. Used by COID Ditch Rider 6/25 
```
This gauge is placed on the crest of the weir (there is a tab at the bottom that holds it in the correct position) and the head at the crest is indicated on the face of the plate. The details of the indications on this gauge plate will be discussed in a subsequent page.
